page: Amazon Web Services a1.4xlarge is a virtualized server instance powered by the first-generation AWS Graviton processor. Built on the ARMv8 architecture using Cortex-A72 cores, this instance features 16 dedicated physical cores and 16 vCPUs running at a clock speed of 2.3 GHz. It is equipped with 32.0 GB of system memory, providing 2.0 GB of RAM per core, and operates on the AWS Nitro hypervisor. The instance does not include local storage or dedicated GPU hardware, relying instead on network-attached storage. Network connectivity is supported by a baseline bandwidth of 5 Gbps.

Benchmark data indicates that the first-generation ARM-based hardware delivers mixed performance characteristics. In CPU-bound tasks, the instance achieves average multi-core performance, as demonstrated by a stress-ng multi-core score of 23,858.27 and a bzip3 multi-threaded decompression speed of 36.86 GB/sec. However, single-core performance is relatively weak, with a Geekbench single-core score of 409.0 and a poor rating in single-threaded gzip compression. Memory performance also shows a stark contrast: cached small-block read bandwidth is average at 566.18 GB/sec, but uncached large-block write bandwidth drops to a poor 10.01 GB/sec.

Given its hardware profile and benchmark results, the a1.4xlarge is best suited for scale-out workloads that can leverage multi-core ARM architectures, such as web serving, containerized microservices, and software compilation. It demonstrates average performance in database operations and Redis SET operations, handling 8.36 million operations per second. However, it is poorly suited for memory-intensive applications, single-threaded tasks, or large language model inference. From a cost-efficiency perspective, the instance offers an entry-point for ARM-based development and testing without the premium associated with newer generation processors, though it trades off raw single-core speed.
description: Amazon Web Services a1.4xlarge is an ARM64-based instance powered by the first-generation AWS Graviton processor with 16 dedicated physical cores and 16 vCPUs running at 2.3 GHz. It features 32.0 GB of system memory, a 5 Gbps baseline network bandwidth, and runs on the Nitro hypervisor without local storage or GPU accelerators. Performance benchmarks show average multi-core capabilities, with a stress-ng multi-core score of 23,858.27 and solid multi-threaded decompression, though single-core performance and uncached memory write bandwidth are weak. This instance is qualitatively cost-effective for scale-out ARM applications, software compilation, and containerized microservices, but is less suitable for memory-intensive or single-threaded workloads.
og_description: Amazon Web Services a1.4xlarge is an ARM64 instance featuring 16 Graviton vCPUs, 32 GB RAM, and a 5 Gbps network baseline, offering cost-effective multi-core performance for scale-out workloads.
meta_description: Amazon Web Services a1.4xlarge is a 16-vCPU ARM64 instance with 32 GB RAM, running on the Nitro hypervisor for scale-out workloads.
tagline: An ARM64-based virtual server featuring sixteen dedicated cores for cost-effective scale-out workloads and multi-threaded application environments.
bullet_points: Powered by first-generation AWS Graviton ARMv8 Cortex-A72 processor
16 dedicated physical cores and 16 vCPUs with 32.0 GB of system memory
5 Gbps baseline network bandwidth running on the Nitro hypervisor
Average multi-core performance for software compilation and database operations
Weak single-core performance and uncached memory write speeds
Best suited for scale-out ARM applications, microservices, and development environments
